Heatwave Alert: Collector Urges Precautions as Temperatures Rise in District
With temperatures rising earlier than expected this summer, District Collector Khushboo Gupta has urged people to remain vigilant and adopt preventive measures to avoid heat stroke and other heat-related illnesses.
Mahabubnagar: With temperatures rising earlier than expected this summer, District Collector Khushboo Gupta has urged people to remain vigilant and adopt preventive measures to avoid heat stroke and other heat-related illnesses.
Chairing a district-level task force committee meeting at the Collectorate on Monday, the Collector highlighted that the increasing intensity of heat poses serious health risks, especially to vulnerable groups such as children, pregnant women, and the elderly. She directed officials to intensify awareness campaigns across the district on precautions to prevent heat stroke.
Emphasizing the need for self-care during extreme weather conditions, she advised people to avoid stepping out during peak afternoon hours and, if necessary, to cover their heads with caps or cloth. She also stressed the importance of staying hydrated by consuming adequate drinking water, ORS, buttermilk, lemon juice, and fruit juices, along with wearing loose and light-colored clothing.
Special instructions were issued to ensure the safety of MGNREGA workers, with directions to provide drinking water and temporary shelters at worksites. Field-level staff, including those from ICDS and other departments, were also asked to take precautions while working in the sun.
The Collector directed health officials to ensure that all government hospitals and Primary Health Centres are equipped with essential medicines and facilities to handle heat-related cases. She also advised people experiencing symptoms such as high fever, dehydration, or dizziness to seek immediate medical attention.
In residential schools for SC, ST, BC, and minority students, officials were instructed to ensure the proper functioning of RO plants and maintain water quality. Students were advised to stay indoors during peak heat hours and avoid consuming outside food, while hostel wardens were directed to provide fresh and hygienic meals.
The administration has also been asked to set up drinking water kiosks at key locations in both urban and rural areas, repair leaking pipelines, and ensure regular cleaning and chlorination of water tanks.
District Medical and Health Officer Dr. K. Krishna warned that prolonged exposure to extreme heat can cause dehydration, high fever, and in severe cases, seizures in children and cardiac complications in adults. He stressed the need for widespread public awareness on recognizing early symptoms and seeking timely treatment.
To mitigate the impact of heat on animals, the Collector directed the Animal Husbandry Department to provide adequate shade and drinking water for livestock, while Forest Department officials were instructed to create water sources in forest areas for wild animals and birds.
On the occasion, the Collector also unveiled a public health advisory poster on heatwave and heat stroke.
